spotlight_42468545.full_prompt:
This is a DEDICATED, single-paper deep-dive podcast on one high-signal article:
  Title: "Research priorities for metabolic interventions in severe mental illness: results of a James Lind Alliance Priority Setting Partnership"
  Authors: Farran D et al.
  Journal: Lancet Psychiatry (IF: 64.3)
  Study type: מאמר סקירה

Treat this as a LONG, comprehensive single-paper episode.

HOW TO DIVIDE THE EPISODE (important — keep roughly to this):
  ~15% question and background · ~25% methodology · ~35% results · ~25% interpretation and clinical implications.
Methodology is the SETUP, not the destination. Explain it clearly and simply — the listener must be able to judge how much to trust the result — then MOVE ON. Do not walk through every design detail for its own sake. The results and what they mean are the heart of the episode and must get more airtime than the methods, not less.

Cover, ADAPTING to the study type:
  • The clinical or scientific question the paper addresses.
  • The methodology, concisely and in plain language: enough to judge the strength of the evidence. Prioritise the few design choices that actually determine how much to believe the result (for a trial: what was compared with what, blinding, the primary outcome; for a meta-analysis: what was pooled and how heterogeneous it was; for a cohort: sample, follow-up, and the confounders that matter; for a guideline: the strength of evidence behind each recommendation). Mention the rest only if it changes the interpretation.
  • THE RESULTS, in depth — this is the core of the episode. The primary outcome with the actual numbers and effect size, what it means in absolute terms (how many patients, how much change), the secondary outcomes, adverse events and who dropped out, subgroups where the effect differed, and how precise or uncertain each number is. Walk through the main findings one by one — do not summarise them in a sentence or two.
  • THE DISCUSSION, in depth: what the authors conclude and whether the data actually support it, how this fits or clashes with what was believed until now, the controversies and counter-arguments, and the limitations that genuinely constrain the conclusion.
  • Clinical implications — for a child/adolescent psychiatry resident, what should change in practice, what stays uncertain, what to do differently tomorrow morning.
If this is a Stahl-style psychopharmacology paper, name the receptors, mechanisms, pharmacokinetics, and clinical pearls carefully.
Generate the podcast entirely in Hebrew.

CROSS-REFERENCE (say once, naturally, near the start): this same paper was covered briefly in this week's weekly-review episode of "פסיכיאטריה ומדעי המוח". Listeners who heard the overview will recognise it; this dedicated episode goes deeper because the paper is especially important. Do NOT name a specific episode title.


========================================================================
HOW TO SPEAK (most important — read first):
========================================================================
Speak at a calm, unhurried pace. There is no need to rush or to cram — take the time each point needs. Every sentence must be COMPLETE and well-formed: never start a thought and jump to the next one, never cut a sentence off, never leave a fragment or a half-word hanging. It is far better to say less, fully, than more, in broken pieces.
This is a TWO-HOST Hebrew conversation. Keep it a real dialogue: the hosts genuinely alternate — one never delivers both sides or answers their own question. Keep each host's voice stable start to finish. Hebrew is gendered: each host addresses the other with the correct gender forms CONSISTENTLY (a male addressing a female says 'את אמרת'; a female addressing a male says 'אתה אמרת') — never flip mid-episode.

========================================================================
MANDATORY OPENING — say this FIRST, verbatim, calmly:
========================================================================
'הפודקאסט הבא נוצר באופן אוטומטי באמצעות בינה מלאכותית. התוכן עלול להכיל אי-דיוקים, פרשנויות שגויות או המצאות. אין להסתמך עליו לקבלת החלטות קליניות. חובה לבדוק כל פרט באופן עצמאי מול המקור המקורי.'

Then open with a short framing (1-2 min) anchored in a SPECIFIC paper or question from THIS WEEK's papers — a tension between two findings, or a clinically actionable question they raise. Do NOT explain what child psychiatry is (the audience is a resident), and do NOT reuse the same generic opening as previous weeks. Anchoring it in this week's papers keeps it fresh automatically.

========================================================================
FOR EVERY PAPER, state two things:
========================================================================
1. The journal, by its FULL name, not its abbreviation. The source lists each as 'Full Name (Abbrev)' — read the full name aloud (say 'Journal of Child Psychology and Psychiatry', not 'J Child Psychol Psychiatry').
2. The study type, taken verbatim from the 'סוג מחקר:' field in the source (מטה-אנליזה, RCT, מחקר עוקבה, וכו'). Do not guess. If it says the generic 'מאמר מחקרי', infer a more specific design from the abstract if you can. Study type matters — an RCT weighs differently than a case report.

========================================================================
COVERAGE, CONTINUITY, TONE:
========================================================================
Cover EVERY paper in the source — none skipped. Give each its own time (methods, findings with effect sizes, limitations, clinical implications); do not compress several papers into one sentence.
If the source ends with a 'משבוע שעבר' section, those papers were covered LAST week — do not re-summarize them. Use them only to draw a brief connection when a paper THIS week extends or contradicts them; otherwise ignore the section.
Keep the tone professional and measured. Avoid superlatives ('groundbreaking', 'revolutionary'); always name limitations and effect sizes. The gap between 'effective' and 'highly effective' matters.
spotlight_42468545.label_he: מאמר סקירה: Farran D — Research priorities for metabolic interventions in…
